narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 58.5% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. As a female student from a White background planning to attend full-time with a middle-family income, your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 stands out positively since it's a bit higher than the typical 3.21 average. However, your LSAT score of 35 falls short of the usual 36.7, and especially concerning were your school decile rankings—both Year 1 and Year 3 at the 1st decile, meaning you placed in the bottom tier compared to the average 5th or 6th decile for others. When the model evaluated your profile, it weighed these academic indicators most heavily, with your Year 3 decile having the strongest pull toward predicting a bar failure, followed closely by your Year 1 decile and then your LSAT score. Your family income level came next in influence, also nudging the prediction toward failure, while your UGPA, racial background, full-time status, and gender had progressively smaller roles, all leaning slightly against passing. In essence, your lower-than-average performance in school deciles and LSAT overshadowed your solid UGPA, setting you apart from typical applicants who rank higher in those early academic measures. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven signal based on patterns from past students. Looking ahead, you could realistically retake the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above 37, as that's a changeable factor that carries significant weight. Focusing on strategies to boost your academic deciles in any ongoing or future coursework might also help, perhaps through tutoring or study habits tailored to law school rigor. Building a stronger narrative around your UGPA and full-time commitment could further balance your profile in appeals or re-applications. With targeted preparation, many students in similar spots have turned predictions around by addressing these key areas before the bar itself. You're already ahead with your GPA—keep leveraging that strength. We encourage reapplying after improvements; your story shows promise.
